questions.stem: In an interview you must pick a structure for frequent random access by index. What do you choose and why?
options: Array — contiguous memory gives O(1) index access
Linked list — pointer chasing is faster for random access
Hash map — always O(1) regardless of key type
Stack — LIFO makes indexing natural
questions.explanation: Arrays (and array-backed lists) offer true O(1) index access via contiguous layout.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why do interviewers care that arrays are contiguous in memory? Which answer should you give?
options: Only to confuse candidates about malloc
Cache locality: sequential scans hit fewer cache lines than pointer-chasing lists
Contiguity makes hashing automatically collision-free
It guarantees O(1) insert at the front
questions.explanation: Spatial locality: adjacent indices share cache lines, so linear scans are cache-friendly.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Java ArrayList / C++ vector growth: what do you say about append cost? Which answer is best?
options: Every append is strictly O(1) forever
Always O(n) because length is recomputed each time
Amortized O(1): occasional resize copies all elements, average stays constant
O(log n) because capacity doubles logarithmically per insert
questions.explanation: Doubling (or geometric growth) makes total copy cost O(n) over n inserts → amortized O(1) per append.

questions.stem: You need to insert at index 0 repeatedly in an ArrayList. What's the honest complexity story?
options: O(1) like a deque
O(log n) via binary search of the hole
Amortized O(1) because capacity doubles
O(n) per insert — elements must shift right
questions.explanation: Front insert shifts every existing element; capacity growth does not fix that.

questions.stem: Fixed-size array vs dynamic list — when do you prefer a raw fixed array in an interview solution?
options: When the max size is known up front and you want zero resize overhead / simpler indexing
Never; dynamic lists are always better
Only when sorting is required
Only for string characters
questions.explanation: Known bound + no resizing = simpler code and predictable memory; common in interview constraints.

questions.stem: Two pointers on a sorted array to find a pair summing to target — why does it work?
options: Because hash maps are illegal on sorted input
Moving left/right based on sum vs target exploits monotonicity of sorted order
It randomly samples until luck finds the pair
It requires the array to be strictly decreasing
questions.explanation: Sorted order lets you decide deterministically whether to increase or decrease the current sum.

questions.stem: What is the classic space/time tradeoff for finding a duplicate in an unsorted array of integers?
options: Only O(n²) nested loops is acceptable
Binary search without sorting always works in O(log n)
Sort in-place O(n log n) time, O(1) extra — or hash set O(n) time, O(n) space
A stack always finds duplicates in O(1) space and O(1) time
questions.explanation: Sort vs hash set is the standard interview tradeoff discussion.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Prefix sums: what problem class do they accelerate? Which answer should you give?
options: Only finding the maximum element
Sorting subarrays in O(1)
Deleting middle elements without shifting
Range sum queries (and related range aggregates) after O(n) preprocess
questions.explanation: prefix[i] = sum(a[0..i]); range sum becomes two lookups.

questions.stem: You say 'sliding window' in an interview. What invariant should you be ready to state?
options: A contiguous subarray whose endpoints move monotonically while maintaining a property (sum, uniqueness, etc.)
The window always covers the entire array
Any subsequence, contiguous or not
A window that only shrinks, never expands
questions.explanation: Sliding window = contiguous segment with advancing L/R pointers under an invariant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Kadane's algorithm — what's the one-sentence intuition you'd give? Which answer should you give?
options: Always take the whole array sum
At each index, decide whether to extend the previous best subarray or start fresh at that index
Use two heaps to track medians of subarrays
Binary search the maximum subarray sum directly
questions.explanation: Local decision: extend vs reset; track the best seen ending-here and global best.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: In-place reverse of an array: which approach do you sketch first? Which answer should you give?
options: Allocate a new array and copy backwards — claim O(1) space
Sort descending then claim it's reversed
Two pointers from ends swapping toward the center
Use a hash map from index to value
questions.explanation: Classic O(n) time, O(1) extra space swap-from-ends.

questions.stem: Dutch National Flag (3-way partition) — when does an interviewer expect it?
options: Any 2-sum problem
Only when the array is already sorted
Finding the median in O(n log n)
Sorting an array of only a few distinct values (e.g. 0/1/2) in one pass
questions.explanation: Three pointers partition into <pivot / =pivot / >pivot regions in linear time.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: You need the k-th largest element. What do you say before coding? Which answer should you give?
options: Full sort O(n log n), or heap/selection (Quickselect) for expected O(n) / O(n log k)
Must fully sort every time — no alternatives
Hashing always gives exact k-th in O(1)
Binary search on indices without values works
questions.explanation: Show you know selection vs sorting tradeoffs.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Sparse array of size 10^9 with few filled cells — what do you propose? Which answer is best?
options: Allocate the full 10^9 int array anyway
Hash map (or compressed sparse structures) keyed by index
Linked list of every integer from 0 to 10^9
A boolean bitset of size 10^9 is always fine in interviews
questions.explanation: Store only occupied indices; dense array blows memory.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Removing an element from the middle of an ArrayList — complexity? Which answer should you give?
options: O(1) always
O(log n) via tree rebalance
O(n) due to shifting subsequent elements
Amortized O(1) like append
questions.explanation: Middle delete shifts the tail left by one each time.

questions.stem: Interview tip: when is an array better than a hash map for membership of integers in [0, n]?
options: Never — hash maps dominate
Only for floating-point keys
Only when order must be preserved by hashing
When the universe is small/dense: a boolean/mark array is O(1) and simpler
questions.explanation: Dense small universe → direct indexing beats hashing overhead.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: What does 'stable sort of an array' mean in an interview? Which answer should you give?
options: Equal elements keep their relative input order
The sort never crashes
The array length stays constant
It runs in O(n) worst case always
questions.explanation: Stability preserves relative order of ties — matters for multi-key sorts.

questions.stem: You rotate an array left by k. What's a clean O(n) time O(1) extra approach?
options: Insert front k times
Reverse whole array, then reverse the two segments defined by k (or three-reverse variant)
Use a hash map of new positions only
Sort then claim rotation happened
questions.explanation: Reversal trick (or juggling) achieves in-place rotation.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why might copying an array of objects be 'shallow' in languages like Java? Which answer is best?
options: Primitive ints are always deep-copied twice
Shallow means the array length is halved
The new array holds the same object references — mutating an element object is visible via both arrays
It implies serialization to disk
questions.explanation: Clone/copy of reference arrays duplicates references, not deep object graphs.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Screening: time to access a[i] in a contiguous array? Which answer should you give?
options: Option claiming: O(i)
O(log n)
Option claiming: O(n)
Option claiming: O(1)
questions.explanation: Address = base + i * stride → constant time.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Maximum sum of a contiguous subarray of length exactly k — best approach? Which answer is best?
options: Sliding window of size k: maintain running sum, slide in O(n)
Kadane without modification
Nested loops only — O(nk) is required
Sort the array then take first k
questions.explanation: Fixed-size window: add right, drop left; O(n) after one pass.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Longest subarray with sum ≤ target (positives only). Pattern? Which answer should you give?
options: Binary search each index independently without window
Expand/shrink sliding window; when sum exceeds target, advance left
Must use segment tree
Dutch flag partition
questions.explanation: Monotonic window works when all values are non-negative.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Two Sum on unsorted array — interview-preferred linear solution? Which answer should you give?
options: Sort then two pointers only — destroys original indices if needed
Triple nested loop
One-pass hash map: value → index while scanning
Build a BST of all prefixes
questions.explanation: Hash complement lookup is the classic O(n) expected approach.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: 3Sum: after sorting, what's the usual next step? Which answer should you give?
options: Three nested binary searches only
Hash every triple into a set without sorting
Convert to linked list first
Fix one index, two-pointer the remaining pair; skip duplicates
questions.explanation: Sort + fix + two pointers is the standard O(n²) interview solution.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Product of array except self without division — key trick? Which answer should you give?
options: Prefix products from left and suffix from right, multiply at each index
Sort then multiply neighbors
Use XOR of all elements
Kadane on logarithms
questions.explanation: Left and right cumulative products avoid division and give O(n).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Find minimum in rotated sorted array (distinct). Approach? Which answer should you give?
options: Linear scan only is the expected answer
Modified binary search comparing mid to high/low to discard a half
Hash the array then take min key
Two pointers from both ends until they meet at max
questions.explanation: Rotated sorted still allows log-time search with careful half discard.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Merge two sorted arrays into one sorted array — complexity you state? Which answer should you give?
options: O(n log n) mandatory merge sort of concatenation
O(1) because both are sorted
O(n+m) two-pointer merge
O(nm) nested compare
questions.explanation: Classic merge step of mergesort: linear in combined length.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Container With Most Water — why two pointers from ends? Which answer should you give?
options: Because height is always bitonic
You must always move both pointers each step
It only works if the array is sorted ascending
Area limited by min height × width; move the shorter line inward seeking a taller wall
questions.explanation: Greedy: width shrinks, so only improving the bottleneck height can help.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Next permutation in-place — high-level steps? Which answer should you give?
options: Find rightmost ascent, swap with next greater from the right, reverse the suffix
Fully reverse then sort randomly
Use a stack of all permutations
Binary search the entire permutation space
questions.explanation: Standard next_permutation algorithm interviewers expect.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Subarray sum equals k (integers may be negative) — which tool? Which answer should you give?
options: Simple two pointers always
Prefix sums + hash map of prefix frequencies
Dutch flag
Only Kadane
questions.explanation: Negatives break sliding window; prefix + hashmap is the go-to.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Move zeros to end while preserving order of non-zeros — clean approach? Which answer is best?
options: Sort the array
Hash set of zero indices
Two pointers / write-index: compact non-zeros forward, fill rest with zero
Recursion on halves
questions.explanation: Single pass compact is O(n) time O(1) extra.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Find the majority element (> n/2). Boyer–Moore vote — what's the claim? Which answer is best?
options: Needs O(n) extra space always
Only works after sorting
Returns all elements that appear twice
O(n) time O(1) space candidate tracking; verify if problem allows non-guaranteed majority
questions.explanation: Pairwise cancellation leaves the majority candidate.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Trapping rain water — interview shape of solution? Which answer should you give?
options: Precompute left/right max height per index, water = min(L,R)−height; or two pointers
Only Monte Carlo simulation
Sort heights then sum differences
DFS on a graph of bars
questions.explanation: Water at i bounded by shorter of tallest bars to left and right.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Maximum product subarray — why is it trickier than Kadane for sums? Which answer should you give?
options: Products never need tracking
Negatives flip min/max; track both best min and max ending here
You must use O(n²) DP only
Sorting fixes products
questions.explanation: Sign flips mean the previous minimum can become the new maximum.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Search a 2D matrix treated as sorted row-wise. Efficient approach? Which answer should you give?
options: Always DFS from (0,0)
Hash every cell into a set first (needed for sorted search)
Flatten mentally and binary search as 1D
Sort each query
questions.explanation: Map mid index to (r,c); binary search over the virtual 1D range.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Spiral matrix traversal — what do you maintain? Which answer should you give?
options: A priority queue of coordinates
Only a single index i
Union-find of visited rows
Boundaries (top/bottom/left/right) shrinking after each layer
questions.explanation: Layer-by-layer with four bounds is the clear interview sketch.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Find first missing positive in O(n) time O(1) extra — idea? Which answer should you give?
options: Index-as-hash: place each value v at index v−1 via swaps, then scan for mismatch
Sort then scan
Hash set only (violates O(1) extra if counted)
Binary search without mutating
questions.explanation: Cycle-sort style placement uses the array as a presence map.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Longest consecutive sequence — O(n) expected approach? Which answer should you give?
options: Sort always O(n log n) is required for O(n) claim
Put nums in a hash set; only start streaks from numbers with no predecessor
Sliding window on the unsorted array directly
Segment tree of gaps
questions.explanation: Set membership + start-of-run check yields amortized O(n).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Merge intervals — steps you recite? Which answer should you give?
options: Hash each interval endpoint randomly
Dutch flag on interval lengths
Sort by start, then sweep merging overlaps into the current open interval
Two pointers without sorting
questions.explanation: Sort then linear merge of overlapping ranges.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Stock buy/sell once — array of prices. Best linear idea? Which answer should you give?
options: Nested compare every pair only
Sort prices ascending then subtract first from last
Prefix XOR
Track min price so far; max of price−minPrice
questions.explanation: One pass: valley so far vs sell today.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Senior: ArrayList capacity doubles vs +10 each resize — why doubling? Which answer should you give?
options: Geometric growth keeps total copy cost O(n); fixed +k makes append amortized O(n)
Doubling looks cooler in diagrams
Doubling uses less peak memory than +10
Language runtimes forbid additive growth
questions.explanation: Amortization requires geometric capacity growth; additive growth is quadratic overall.

questions.stem: Cache-aware: why might iterating row-major beat column-major on a C-style 2D array?
options: CPUs reverse columns automatically
Row-major layout places row neighbors in consecutive addresses → better prefetch/cache
Column-major always faults less
It only matters for linked lists
questions.explanation: Match access order to physical layout for locality.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Pitfall: sliding window for 'sum = k' with negatives — why does it break? Which answer is best?
options: Windows cannot contain negatives by definition
Hash maps cannot store negative keys
Sum is no longer monotonic when expanding/shrinking, so the usual left-move rule is invalid
Two pointers require the array to be a palindrome
questions.explanation: Monotonicity of the window sum is the hidden assumption.

questions.stem: In-place vs extra array for rotate/reverse — what do you discuss with interviewer?
options: In-place always wins on time complexity asymptotically
Extra space is always slower by Θ(n)
Interviewers never care about mutating input
In-place saves memory and can be cache-friendlier, but mutates input (side effects, concurrency, reuse)
questions.explanation: Correctness of mutation, API contract, and memory — not just Big-O.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Quickselect for k-th: what's the honest worst-case story? Which answer should you give?
options: Expected O(n) with random pivots; adversarial pivots can degrade to O(n²) unless mitigated
Always O(n) worst case like mergesort
Always O(n log n)
Worse than full sort in every case
questions.explanation: Average great; worst-case needs care (randomization / median-of-medians).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: False friend: 'O(1) index access' on a Java List — when is that wrong? Which answer is best?
options: Never; List guarantees it
LinkedList implements List but get(i) is O(n); ArrayList is O(1)
Only ArrayList is O(n)
HashMap.get is O(n) for integers
questions.explanation: Interface ≠ implementation cost; know the concrete class.

questions.stem: Integer overflow when computing mid = (lo+hi)/2 in binary search on arrays — fix?
options: Use floating point mid
Always start lo at 1
mid = lo + (hi−lo)/2 (or language-safe alternatives)
Disable bounds checks
questions.explanation: Avoid lo+hi overflow; classic interview footgun.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: When does prefix-sum of ints become a correctness hazard? Which answer should you give?
options: Never for 32-bit arrays under size 10
Prefix sums cannot represent negative numbers
They only fail on even lengths
Large n / large values: partial sums overflow; use wider type or modular arithmetic as required
questions.explanation: Silent overflow breaks range-sum answers.

questions.stem: Off-by-one in sliding window 'at most k distinct' — what do seniors double-check?
options: Inclusive bounds, when to advance left, and whether answer uses length r−l+1 vs r−l
Whether k is prime
Only the hash map load factor
Endianness of array indices
questions.explanation: Window length and move-left timing are the usual bugs.

questions.stem: Tradeoff: sort+two-pointers vs hash for Two Sum when you also need original indices?
options: Sort always preserves indices
Hashing keeps indices naturally; sorting needs index pairs and careful tie handling
Neither can recover indices
Two pointers without sort recover indices in O(1)
questions.explanation: Sorting permutes positions — track indices explicitly or prefer a map.
